questions.question: What does CGI stand for in the context of web servers?
options: Content Generation Interface
Common Gateway Interface
Client GUI Integration
Computer Graphics Interchange
questions.explanation: CGI stands for Common Gateway Interface (RFC 3875). It is a standard protocol that defines how a web server communicates with an external program (the CGI script) to generate dynamic web content.

questions.question: When a browser loads a page built with client-side rendering (CSR), what does the server initially send?
options: A fully rendered HTML page with all data already embedded
A minimal HTML skeleton and a JavaScript bundle that fetches data separately
Only a JSON file with no HTML at all
A PDF representation of the page content
questions.explanation: In CSR (used by React, Vue, Angular), the server sends a skeleton HTML page plus a JavaScript bundle. The JS then runs in the browser, calls JSON APIs, and builds the DOM dynamically. The server does not generate the full HTML.

questions.question: In CGI/1.1 (RFC 3875), how does a web server deliver a POST request's form body to the CGI script?
options: As a command-line argument to the script process
Via the QUERY_STRING environment variable
Via the script's standard input (stdin), with CONTENT_LENGTH bytes to read
In a temporary file whose path is set in the PATH_INFO variable
questions.explanation: RFC 3875 §4.1.2 and §4.2 specify that POST body data is delivered to the CGI script via stdin. The script must read exactly CONTENT_LENGTH bytes. QUERY_STRING is used only for GET parameters (the part after '?' in the URL).

questions.question: Which CGI environment variable (RFC 3875 §4.1.7) holds the URL-encoded parameters that appear after the '?' in the request URL?
options: PATH_INFO
CONTENT_TYPE
QUERY_STRING
SCRIPT_NAME
questions.explanation: RFC 3875 §4.1.7 defines QUERY_STRING as the part of the request URL after the '?' character. It is URL-encoded (percent-encoded) and must be decoded by the CGI script using a parser such as urllib.parse.parse_qs().

questions.question: What is the primary performance problem with traditional CGI/1.1 compared to FastCGI or WSGI?
options: CGI can only handle HTTP GET requests, not POST
CGI requires the client to re-authenticate on every request
CGI forks and exec()s a new OS process for every HTTP request, creating high startup overhead
CGI does not support environment variables, so data must be passed via temporary files
questions.explanation: The core CGI/1.1 model (RFC 3875) requires the server to fork() a new process and exec() the script interpreter for every request. On a Python CGI script this costs ~15 ms per request just for interpreter startup. FastCGI keeps persistent worker processes; WSGI calls a Python callable directly — neither pays the fork/exec cost.

questions.question: A development team wants excellent SEO for their product listing pages and is comfortable with full page reloads on navigation. Which rendering approach best fits their needs?
options: Client-side rendering (CSR) with React and a JSON API
Server-side rendering (SSR) with a framework like Django or PHP
Hybrid rendering (Next.js) with client-side hydration
A static CDN with no server-side code at all
questions.explanation: Server-side rendering (SSR) — CGI, PHP, JSP, Django templates, etc. — generates complete HTML on the server. This means search engine crawlers receive fully rendered content (good for SEO) and pages are accessible without JavaScript. The trade-off is a full page reload on each navigation, which is acceptable in this scenario.
